retrieved_docs.title: Benzodiazepines -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: Midazolam: Midazolam is administered 10 mg IM for convulsive status epilepticus as a single dose. Midazolam is preferred for patients without IV access. Intranasal midazolam (0.2 mg/kg, maximum dose, 10 mg) can be used in prehospital settings. For procedural sedation in patients <60, 0.5 to 2.5 mg IV should be administered slowly over at least 2 minutes. Clinicians should fully evaluate the sedative effect before administering another dose. In MICU/SICU, midazolam is used as an alternative agent for sedation in mechanically ventilated patients. An intermittent dosing strategy is preferred to avoid drug accumulation. The Richmond Agitation Sedation Scale (RASS) goal is 0 to −2. [11] [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Midazolam -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: Midazolam has poor oral absorption. Midazolam is rapidly and efficiently absorbed through various routes of administration. Intramuscular administration has a bioavailability greater than 90%, with peak plasma concentration (Tmax) typically reached within 30 minutes. Intranasal administration results in rapid systemic absorption, with a Tmax of approximately 10 to 15 minutes, making it suitable for emergent use. Buccal administration is also effective, with a Tmax of roughly 25 to 30 minutes, and is commonly used in pediatric seizure settings. Intravenous administration provides immediate systemic availability, with Tmax occurring within minutes. The active metabolite, 1-hydroxy-midazolam, reaches peak plasma concentrations approximately 1 hour after intramuscular injection.

retrieved_docs.title: Midazolam -- Administration -- Injection (IV/IM)
retrieved_docs.content: 1 mg/mL 5 mg/mL 10 mg/0.7 mL (autoinjector)

retrieved_docs.title: Midazolam -- Administration -- Oral Syrup
retrieved_docs.content: Typically 2 mg/mL

retrieved_docs.title: Midazolam -- Administration -- Nasal Spray
retrieved_docs.content: 5 mg per 0.1 mL spray

retrieved_docs.title: Midazolam -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Midazolam is a short and rapidly acting benzodiazepine. Intravenous midazolam is used for the induction of anesthesia and also in the management of acute seizures. Because of its water-soluble nature, midazolam has a rapid onset of action and can be used to manage status epilepticus when intravenous administration of other medications is not feasible. Midazolam has a high rate of tolerance, and the dose can be increased to maintain the therapeutic effect. Because of its easy mode of administration through the buccal and intranasal routes, it is a viable option in children to manage seizures. For its use in anesthesia, the response to the induction dose is more variable than that of thiopental. Midazolam can be used for anxiolysis and hypnosis during the maintenance phase of general anesthesia and is also superior to thiopental in maintaining anesthesia due to its reduced need for adjunct medications. Midazolam is an adjunctive medication used in conjunction with regional and local anesthesia for various diagnostic and therapeutic procedures, and it is more widely accepted by both patients and physicians. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Midazolam -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Midazolam is a short-acting benzodiazepine frequently used for anesthesia induction, seizure management, and procedural sedation. The water-soluble formulation allows for rapid onset and flexible administration routes, including intravenous and intramuscular, making it suitable for managing status epilepticus when intravenous access is limited. Midazolam also provides anxiolysis and hypnosis during the maintenance phase of general anesthesia and serves as an adjunct to regional and local anesthesia in various diagnostic and therapeutic procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Midazolam -- Administration -- Available Dosage Forms and Strengths
retrieved_docs.content: Midazolam can be administered through oral, intranasal, buccal, intravenous, and intramuscular routes. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Midazolam -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The separate parenteral formulation is approved for the treatment of status epilepticus. According to the American Epilepsy Society guidelines, in adults with status epilepticus who do not have established IV access, IM midazolam is considered more effective than IV lorazepam (Level A). No significant difference in effectiveness has been demonstrated between lorazepam and diazepam in adults with status epilepticus (Level A). In children, IV diazepam and IV lorazepam are shown to be effective at stopping seizures lasting at least 5 minutes (Level A). Rectal diazepam, IM midazolam, intranasal midazolam, and buccal midazolam are likely effective at stopping seizures lasting at least 5 minutes (Level B). According to the American Epilepsy Society, treatment begins with a stabilization phase that lasts for the first 5 minutes, which includes specific initial seizure first aid management, including protecting the airway, maintaining breathing, and ensuring circulation. Benzodiazepines such as IM midazolam, IV lorazepam, or IV diazepam are recommended as the initial therapy of choice, given their demonstrated efficacy and safety. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Procedural Sedation -- Technique or Treatment -- Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Midazolam takes effect within 1 to 5 minutes when administered intravenously and within 10 minutes when administered intranasally. The peak effect occurs within 5 to 10 minutes and can last up to 60 minutes when given intravenously, whereas the nasal route provides effects lasting 20 to 25 minutes.

retrieved_docs.title: Conscious Sedation in Dentistry -- Technique or Treatment -- Intravenous Sedation with Midazolam
retrieved_docs.content: Midazolam is commonly stored in a 5-mg vial drawn up with a needle. The drug is titrated, ensuring it is given in a controlled manner so that the patient can be monitored closely to mitigate further the risk of respiratory depression, over-sedation, or other complications. An accepted titration method is the slow injection of 2 mg of midazolam, a 90-second pause. Then further 1 mg doses at 1-minute intervals until a safe and satisfactory level of sedation is achieved. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Procedural Sedation -- Technique or Treatment -- Medications
retrieved_docs.content: The ideal agent for procedural sedation and analgesia should provide sedative, analgesic, and amnestic effects while ensuring a rapid onset and short duration of action. These properties support safe, efficient recovery and timely discharge. Procedural sedation and analgesia commonly involve combining a short-acting benzodiazepine, such as midazolam, with an opioid, such as fentanyl. Midazolam provides sedative, amnestic, and anxiolytic effects but lacks analgesic properties, which fentanyl supplements. However, several other medications may be used alone or in combination. [14] Short-acting agents such as propofol or etomidate are suitable for sedation in healthy patients. When intravenous access is unavailable, intranasal midazolam and intranasal fentanyl offer alternative options.

retrieved_docs.title: Benzodiazepines -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric patients: Per the American Epilepsy Society guidelines, the IV administration of lorazepam and diazepam effectively stops seizures lasting at least 5 minutes. Additionally, rectal diazepam, IM midazolam, intranasal midazolam, and buccal midazolam are probably effective. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Midazolam -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: For the perioperative use of midazolam, the induction dose is 0.15 to 0.40 mg/kg via the intravenous route. The premedication dose is 0.07 to 0.10 mg/kg administered intramuscularly. The dose is titrated at 0.05 to 0.15 mg/kg for intravenous sedation. For children aged 1 to 5 months, the recommended intranasal dose is 0.2 mg/kg. For children 6 months and older, the recommended dose is 0.2 to 0.3 mg/kg intranasally. [1] For status epilepticus, 10 mg midazolam for a weight greater than 40 kg is recommended. The American Epilepsy Society also notes in the treatment algorithm that the dose should be reduced to 4 mg IM in patients weighing 13 kg to 40 kg. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Procedural Sedation -- Technique or Treatment -- Medications
retrieved_docs.content: When administered intravenously, the initial dose ranges from 0.5 to 2.5 mg, with repeat dosing every 2 to 5 minutes as required. In healthy young adults, total doses exceeding 5 mg are rarely required, whereas debilitated and older patients typically need less than 3.5 mg. When given intranasally, the dose is 0.1 mg/kg, with a maximum single dose of 10 mg, administered 15 to 20 minutes before the procedure. The dose should be divided equally between both nostrils. Midazolam can cause a burning sensation in the nose, which may be reduced by using an atomizer for delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: Benzodiazepines -- Administration -- Available Dosage Forms and Strengths
retrieved_docs.content: Benzodiazepines are commonly administered via the oral and IV routes. As protocol dictates, they may also be administered rectally, intranasally, and IM. For instance, intranasal or IM administration may be useful in actively seizing patients where IV or oral administration cannot be safely performed. Rectal administration in pediatric patients may be used for seizure cessation when IV access has not been established.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1467
retrieved_docs.content: Bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics. These agents belong to the benzodiazepine family and share a common mechanism of enhancing the inhibitory effects of gamma‑aminobutyric acid (GABA) at the GABA‑A receptor, which produces anxiolytic, sedative, muscle‑relaxant, and anticonvulsant effects. Because they are members of the same therapeutic class, they are often considered together when selecting medication for anxiety disorders, procedural sedation, or related conditions, and their pharmacologic similarities guide dosing, monitoring, and the management of potential side‑effects in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 814
retrieved_docs.content: 7‑nitroindazole, bromazepam, buspirone, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam, prazepam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics. Anxiolytics constitute a therapeutic class of drugs used to reduce anxiety symptoms, and each of these compounds is recognized as belonging to that class. Although they differ in chemical structure and specific clinical applications, they share the common purpose of alleviating anxiety, and selection among them in patient care depends on factors such as efficacy, side‑effect profile, and individual patient needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1345
retrieved_docs.content: Midazolam, a benzodiazepine, is contraindicated for co‑administration with the antiretroviral protease inhibitor saquinavir because of a drug‑class interaction that can increase adverse effects. Similarly, saquinavir is contraindicated for use together with the benzodiazepine triazolam, reflecting the same class‑based incompatibility. These contraindications highlight the need to avoid combining saquinavir with benzodiazepines such as midazolam or triazolam in patient care to prevent potentially serious drug‑interaction–related toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1678
retrieved_docs.content: Nimodipine and midazolam are both identified as contraindicated when used together with the antiretroviral medication saquinavir, indicating that coadministration of saquinavir with either nimodipine or midazolam should be avoided due to the risk of significant drug‑drug interactions. Clinicians should be aware of these contraindications to prevent adverse effects and ensure safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1987
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiolytics constitute a therapeutic class that includes several benzodiazepine agents—bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam—as well as the non‑benzodiazepine anxiolytic buspiron. All of these drugs share the primary function of reducing anxiety symptoms, though they differ in pharmacokinetic profiles, potency, and specific clinical indications. Benzodiazepines such as bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam act by enhancing the inhibitory effects of gamma‑aminobutyric acid (GABA) at the GABA‑A receptor, leading to rapid anxiolysis, sedation, muscle relaxation, and anticonvulsant effects. Buspiron, in contrast, exerts its anxiolytic action primarily through partial agonism at serotonin 5‑HT1A receptors, offering anxiety relief with a lower risk of sedation and dependence. Recognizing that these diverse agents all belong to the anxiolytic category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on the patient’s specific needs, risk of side effects, and potential for dependence.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: 7‑nitroindazole, PD 134308, alpidem, bromazepam, buspiron, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam, prazepam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytic agents. Each of these compounds belongs to the broader class of anxiolytics, which are medications used to alleviate anxiety symptoms. Recognizing that these diverse chemical entities share the same therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate anxiolytic therapy based on individual drug properties, pharmacokinetics, and patient-specific factors, thereby optimizing anxiety management and overall patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2040
retrieved_docs.content: identified as an opioid analgesic, highlighting the therapeutic potential of kappa‑receptor targeting agents, which may offer analgesia with a different side‑effect profile compared with traditional mu‑agonists. Collectively, these entities demonstrate that opioid analgesics encompass a broad spectrum of dosage forms (injectable, oral solution, capsule), combination products, and both classic and investigational opioid molecules, all aimed at providing effective pain control across various clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1686
retrieved_docs.content: Bromazepam, buspirone, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, prazepam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics, a therapeutic group of medications used to alleviate anxiety symptoms. As members of the same drug class, these agents share the primary indication of treating anxiety disorders, although they differ in chemical structure, pharmacokinetics and side‑effect profiles, which influences the choice of a specific anxiolytic for individual patient care. Understanding that each of these drugs belongs to the anxiolytic category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, monitor for class‑related adverse effects, and provide comprehensive management of anxiety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1084
retrieved_docs.content: Alprazolam, a benzodiazepine anxiolytic, has important contraindications with several agents. It is contraindicated in combination with the triazole antifungal itrakonazol and with the protease inhibitor indinavir, including its specific formulations indinavir anhydrous and indinavir‑sulfát. Both itrakonazol and vorikonazol belong to the pharmacologic class “Triazole derivatives, systemic antimycotics,” indicating that they share similar mechanisms of action as systemic antifungal agents. Consequently, co‑administration of alprazolam with drugs from the triazole systemic antimycotic class (such as itrakonazol and vorikonazol) or with indinavir in any of its forms should be avoided to prevent adverse drug‑drug interactions. Awareness of these contraindications is essential for clinicians to ensure safe prescribing practices and to protect patients receiving antiretroviral therapy or systemic triazole antifungal treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 0 -- Community 8
retrieved_docs.content: clinicians with a structured framework for selecting appropriate bronchodilators, systemic adrenergics, mineralocorticoids and diagnostic tools based on receptor selectivity, route of administration, therapeutic group, contraindications and combination formulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1557
retrieved_docs.content: sulfate concentrations and delivery formats provide flexibility for clinicians to tailor dosing to individual patient needs, ranging from low‑dose prefilled syringes for controlled administration to higher‑concentration solutions for more intensive pain management. Collectively, this diverse group of opioid analgesics illustrates the breadth of pharmacologic options available for effective pain control across different clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1319
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiolytics comprise a diverse group of medications used to alleviate anxiety, and several specific agents are classified within this category. Bromazepam, buspiron, demethyldiazepam, and estazolam are all identified as anxiolytic drugs. In addition, medazepam, midazolam, prazepam, and triazolam are likewise recognized as members of the anxiolytic class. Together, these agents represent a range of pharmacologic options—primarily benzodiazepine derivatives and a non‑benzodiazepine anxiolytic (buspiron)—that clinicians may select to manage anxiety disorders and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2197
retrieved_docs.content: the therapeutic relevance of targeting specific opioid receptor subtypes. Collectively, these entities demonstrate the extensive pharmacologic diversity of opioid analgesics, ranging from traditional mu‑receptor agonists to kappa‑selective compounds, and underscore the importance of formulation choice (injection, oral capsule, oral solution, prefilled syringe) in optimizing pain management and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2049
retrieved_docs.content: PD 134308, bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, honokiol, midazolam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics. As members of the anxiolytic drug class, each of these agents is used to alleviate anxiety symptoms, and they share pharmacologic properties typical of medications that act on the central nervous system to produce calming effects. Recognizing that these diverse compounds—ranging from benzodiazepine derivatives such as bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam and triazolam to the natural product honokiol and the investigational compound PD 134308—belong to the same therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate anxiety‑reducing treatments and understand their common mechanisms of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: with ketoconazole, indicating that co‑administration may precipitate adverse effects. Conivaptan hydrochloride is also contraindicated with ketoconazole, and ketoconazole itself is contraindicated with ubrogepant. These contraindications underscore the need for clinicians to review a patient’s medication regimen before prescribing ketoconazole‑containing products, particularly when the patient is receiving ergotamine derivatives, vasopressin antagonists, or CGRP receptor antagonists.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1640
retrieved_docs.content: Diazepam is a centrally acting myorelaxant, and its primary metabolic site is hepatic metabolism, indicating that the liver is responsible for processing this medication. Recognizing that hepatic metabolism is the metabolic site of diazepam is important for understanding its pharmacokinetic behavior, potential drug interactions, and dosing considerations, especially in patients with liver dysfunction.
